questions.stem: In the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model $\dot{x} = ax - bxy$, $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy$ (prey $x$, predator $y$, with $a,b,c,d > 0$), the term $-bxy$ in the prey equation represents
questions.explanation: The product $xy$ measures the rate of predator-prey encounters. The term $-bxy$ removes prey at a rate proportional to encounters, so it is the loss of prey to predation. The matching gain to predators is $+dxy$ in the predator equation.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the Lotka-Volterra system $\dot{x} = ax - bxy$, $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy$, the nontrivial (coexistence) equilibrium $(x^*, y^*)$ is
questions.explanation: Set both rates to zero away from the origin. From $ax - bxy = x(a - by) = 0$ the nonzero prey gives $y^* = \frac{a}{b}$. From $-cy + dxy = y(-c + dx) = 0$ the nonzero predator gives $x^* = \frac{c}{d}$. So $(x^*, y^*) = \left(\frac{c}{d}, \frac{a}{b}\right)$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the prey equation $\dot{x} = ax - bxy = 0$, the prey nullcline (other than $x = 0$) is
questions.explanation: Factor: $x(a - by) = 0$. Apart from the axis $x = 0$, the prey nullcline is $a - by = 0$, that is $y = \frac{a}{b}$, a horizontal line in the phase plane. Along it the prey momentarily stops changing.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the predator equation $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy = 0$, the predator nullcline (other than $y = 0$) is
questions.explanation: Factor: $y(-c + dx) = 0$. Apart from $y = 0$, the predator nullcline is $-c + dx = 0$, that is $x = \frac{c}{d}$, a vertical line. Along it the predator population momentarily stops changing.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The Jacobian of the Lotka-Volterra system, evaluated at the coexistence equilibrium $(c/d, a/b)$, is
questions.explanation: The Jacobian is $J = \begin{pmatrix} a - by & -bx \\ dy & -c + dx \end{pmatrix}$. At $(x^*,y^*) = (c/d, a/b)$ the diagonal entries vanish: $a - by^* = a - a = 0$ and $-c + dx^* = -c + c = 0$. The off-diagonal entries are $-bx^* = -bc/d$ and $dy^* = da/b$, giving $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -bc/d \\ da/b & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: At the Lotka-Volterra coexistence equilibrium the Jacobian has trace $0$ and determinant $ac > 0$. Its eigenvalues are therefore
options: real and of opposite sign (a saddle)
real and both negative (a stable node)
complex with negative real part (a stable spiral)
questions.explanation: The eigenvalues satisfy $\lambda^2 - (\text{tr})\lambda + \det = 0$, i.e. $\lambda^2 + ac = 0$, so $\lambda = \pm i\sqrt{ac}$. They are purely imaginary, making the equilibrium a center: the linearization predicts neutrally stable closed orbits around it.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The classic behaviour of Lotka-Volterra predator-prey dynamics in the phase plane is
options: both populations spiral into a stable steady state
closed orbits, so the populations oscillate periodically
questions.explanation: Because the coexistence equilibrium is a center, trajectories are closed loops surrounding it. Predator and prey numbers therefore rise and fall in a repeating cycle, with the predator peak lagging the prey peak. The amplitude is set by the initial conditions.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Consider Lotka-Volterra with $a = 1$, $b = 0.1$, $c = 0.5$, $d = 0.02$ (so $\dot{x} = x - 0.1xy$, $\dot{y} = -0.5y + 0.02xy$). The coexistence equilibrium $(x^*, y^*)$ is
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For small oscillations about the Lotka-Volterra equilibrium with eigenvalues $\pm i\sqrt{ac}$, the period of the cycle is
questions.explanation: For oscillations of angular frequency $\omega$ the period is $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$. Here $\omega = \sqrt{ac}$ (the imaginary part of the eigenvalues), so $T = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{ac}}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Using the data $a = 1$, $c = 0.5$, the period of small Lotka-Volterra oscillations $T = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{ac}}$ is approximately
questions.explanation: $\sqrt{ac} = \sqrt{1 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.707$. Then $T = \frac{2\pi}{0.707} = \frac{6.283}{0.707} = 8.89$. So the period is about $8.9$ time units.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey cycle, the predator population peaks
options: at the same time as the prey
exactly out of phase, when prey is lowest
questions.explanation: Prey abundance fuels predator growth, so predators continue to increase while prey are plentiful and only peak once prey have begun to fall. The predator maximum therefore lags the prey maximum by roughly a quarter of a cycle.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The Lotka-Volterra competition model is $\dot{N_1} = r_1 N_1\left(1 - \frac{N_1 + \alpha_{12} N_2}{K_1}\right)$, $\dot{N_2} = r_2 N_2\left(1 - \frac{N_2 + \alpha_{21} N_1}{K_2}\right)$. The coefficient $\alpha_{12}$ measures
options: the competitive effect of species 2 on species 1
the competitive effect of species 1 on species 2
questions.explanation: In species 1's equation, $\alpha_{12} N_2$ converts individuals of species 2 into equivalent units of species 1 crowding. Thus $\alpha_{12}$ is the per capita competitive effect of species 2 on species 1. The reverse effect is $\alpha_{21}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the competition model, setting $\dot{N_1} = 0$ gives (apart from $N_1 = 0$) the species-1 nullcline. It is the straight line
questions.explanation: From $r_1 N_1\left(1 - \frac{N_1 + \alpha_{12} N_2}{K_1}\right) = 0$ with $N_1 \neq 0$, the bracket vanishes: $\frac{N_1 + \alpha_{12} N_2}{K_1} = 1$, i.e. $N_1 + \alpha_{12} N_2 = K_1$. This line has intercepts $K_1$ on the $N_1$-axis and $K_1/\alpha_{12}$ on the $N_2$-axis.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In Lotka-Volterra competition, stable coexistence of both species (a stable interior equilibrium) requires the two zero-growth nullclines to
options: never intersect in the positive quadrant
intersect with intraspecific competition dominating interspecific competition
questions.explanation: A stable interior equilibrium exists when each species limits itself more than it limits the other, which geometrically makes the nullclines cross so that each species' carrying-capacity intercept lies inside the other's. The condition is $\alpha_{12}\alpha_{21} < 1$ (intraspecific competition stronger than interspecific). Otherwise one species drives the other out.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The principle of competitive exclusion, as illustrated by the Lotka-Volterra competition model, states that
options: competition always drives both species extinct
two species competing for the same limiting resource cannot coexist indefinitely; one excludes the other
the species with the larger growth rate always wins
questions.explanation: When interspecific competition is strong (each species suppresses the other more than itself), the interior equilibrium is unstable and the outcome depends on initial conditions, with one species ultimately excluding the other. This is the competitive exclusion principle: complete competitors cannot coexist indefinitely.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Two competing species follow the Lotka-Volterra competition model with $K_1 = 100$, $\alpha_{12} = 0.5$. The species-1 nullcline $N_1 + \alpha_{12} N_2 = K_1$ crosses the $N_2$-axis (where $N_1 = 0$) at
questions.explanation: Set $N_1 = 0$: $\alpha_{12} N_2 = K_1$, so $N_2 = \frac{K_1}{\alpha_{12}} = \frac{100}{0.5} = 200$. (The same line meets the $N_1$-axis at $N_1 = K_1 = 100$.)
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A mutualism (facilitation) interaction between two species, in contrast to competition, is modelled by interaction terms that are
options: negative for one and positive for the other
questions.explanation: Mutualism benefits both partners, so each species' per capita growth rate increases with the abundance of the other: the cross terms enter with positive sign for both equations. Competition has them negative for both, and predator-prey has one positive (predator) and one negative (prey).
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A more realistic predator-prey model replaces the prey growth term $ax$ with logistic prey growth $ax(1 - x/K)$. The main qualitative effect on the coexistence equilibrium is that it
options: remains a neutral center as before
can become a stable spiral, so oscillations damp out toward a steady state
questions.explanation: Adding prey self-limitation introduces damping. The pure imaginary eigenvalues of the classic model acquire a negative real part, turning the neutral center into a stable spiral (or node). Trajectories then spiral inward and the oscillations decay toward the coexistence steady state rather than persisting undamped.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The origin $(0,0)$ of the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey system $\dot{x} = ax - bxy$, $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy$ is
questions.explanation: The Jacobian at $(0,0)$ is $\begin{pmatrix} a & 0 \\ 0 & -c \end{pmatrix}$, with eigenvalues $a > 0$ and $-c < 0$. Eigenvalues of opposite sign make the origin a saddle: prey grows along one direction while predators decay along the other.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the predator-prey phase plane, the two nullclines $y = a/b$ (prey) and $x = c/d$ (predator) intersect at
questions.explanation: The horizontal prey nullcline $y = a/b$ and the vertical predator nullcline $x = c/d$ meet where both populations are simultaneously steady, namely the single point $(c/d, a/b)$, which is exactly the coexistence equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A functional response in which a predator's intake of prey saturates at high prey density (the Holling type II form) is written as
questions.explanation: The Holling type II functional response $\frac{a x}{1 + a h x}$ rises linearly at low prey density $x$ but levels off to $1/h$ as $x \to \infty$, reflecting the handling time $h$ that limits how fast a predator can process prey. The linear form $ax$ (type I) never saturates.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Consider the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model $\dot{x} = ax - bxy$, $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy$ with $a = 2$, $b = 0.5$, $c = 1$, $d = 0.1$.
questions.solution: The coexistence equilibrium has prey value $x^* = c/d =$ {{1}} and predator value $y^* = a/b =$ {{2}}. Because the Jacobian there has zero trace and positive determinant, the equilibrium is a {{3}}, so the populations cycle indefinitely.
questions.explanation: $x^* = c/d = 1/0.1 = 10$ and $y^* = a/b = 2/0.5 = 4$. With trace $0$ and determinant $ac = 2 > 0$, the eigenvalues are purely imaginary, so the equilibrium is a center surrounded by closed orbits.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Analyze the nullclines of $\dot{x} = ax - bxy$, $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy$.
questions.solution: Setting $\dot{x} = 0$ away from $x = 0$ gives the prey nullcline, which is a horizontal line at $y = a/b$; this means the variable held constant along it is the {{1}} population's level. Setting $\dot{y} = 0$ away from $y = 0$ gives the predator nullcline, the vertical line $x = c/d$. The two nullclines cross at exactly {{2}} interior point, which is the coexistence equilibrium.
questions.explanation: The prey nullcline $y = a/b$ fixes the predator level $y$; the predator nullcline $x = c/d$ fixes the prey level $x$. A horizontal line and a vertical line meet at exactly one point, the equilibrium $(c/d, a/b)$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Two species obey the Lotka-Volterra competition model with $K_1 = 120$, $K_2 = 80$, $\alpha_{12} = 2$, $\alpha_{21} = 0.8$.
questions.solution: The product of competition coefficients is $\alpha_{12}\alpha_{21} =$ {{1}}. Since this value is greater than $1$, interspecific competition is strong, the interior equilibrium is {{2}}, and the long term outcome is competitive {{3}} of one species.
questions.explanation: $\alpha_{12}\alpha_{21} = 2 \times 0.8 = 1.6 > 1$. Interspecific competition exceeds intraspecific, so the interior equilibrium is a saddle (unstable) and the system shows competitive exclusion, where the winner depends on initial conditions.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Classify the type of ecological interaction from the signs of the per capita effect each species has on the other.
questions.solution: If each species increases the other's growth rate (both cross effects positive), the interaction is {{1}}. If each species decreases the other's growth rate (both cross effects negative), it is competition. If one effect is positive and the other negative, as when one species eats the other, it is a {{2}} interaction.
questions.explanation: Both positive cross effects define mutualism; both negative define competition; one positive and one negative (predator gains, prey loses) define a predator-prey interaction.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the Lotka-Volterra model with $a = 4$ and $c = 1$, study the small oscillations about the coexistence equilibrium.
questions.solution: The eigenvalues of the linearization are $\pm i\sqrt{ac}$, so the angular frequency of the cycle is $\omega = \sqrt{ac} =$ {{1}}. The period is then $T = 2\pi/\omega \approx$ {{2}} time units (to one decimal place).
questions.explanation: $\omega = \sqrt{ac} = \sqrt{4 \times 1} = 2$. The period is $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{6.283}{2} = 3.14 \approx 3.1$ time units.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Examine the origin $(0,0)$ of the predator-prey system $\dot{x} = ax - bxy$, $\dot{y} = -cy + dxy$.
questions.solution: Linearizing at the origin gives a diagonal Jacobian with eigenvalues $a$ and $-c$. With $a > 0$ and $c > 0$ these have opposite signs, so the origin is a {{1}}. Along the prey axis (predators absent) the prey grows {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Eigenvalues $a > 0$ and $-c < 0$ of opposite sign make the origin a saddle. With $y = 0$ the prey equation reduces to $\dot{x} = ax$, pure Malthusian growth, so prey increases exponentially.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology
